Case Study · 01 An AI Interaction Study · 2026 Rajatpal S Sodhi

An AI
that grows
with you,
not for you.

A case study on designing reflective friction, epistemic transparency, and a new metric for AI: cognitive growth.

Rajatpal S Sodhi Humane Design 2026
Scroll
RoleSole designer & researcher
Timeline10 weeks · 2026
DisciplineMindful Interactions
ToolsFigma · Cursor · Antigravity · Gemini 2.5
MethodPersona-based usability walkthroughs
OutputWorking prototype + 7-flaw audit
01 — The Tension
01— The Tension

The blank page
became frightening.

Somewhere along the way, I noticed I'd open an AI tool before I'd finished reading the brief — not because I was stuck, but because the silence had become unbearable. The muscle for original thought had gone quiet from disuse.

I made the problem itself the thesis: the cognitive debt of generative AI.

It started as a research argument: frictionless AI creates a measurable cognitive debt — not in the future, now. That argument became a working prototype. This case study put the prototype in front of five real people whose relationship with AI is shaped by real-life pressures, not academic curiosity.

I built Mentor not for designers. I built it for everyone who depends on AI and deserves better from it.

— Standard AI

"Faster is always better"

Frictionless. Confident. Dependency-creating.

— Mentor

"Cognitive growth over speed"

Scaffolded. Transparent. Regenerative.

02 — The Thesis
02— Three Principles

The architecture
of cognitive care.

Every decision in Mentor — from copy to micro-interactions to the business model — is anchored in three principles. They are not features. They are the design's spine.

Ask Before Answer/ Show the Seams/ Grow With You/ Ask Before Answer/ Show the Seams/ Grow With You/
01

Ask Before Answer

Before the AI generates, the user shares their instinct. The tool builds on what the user already knows rather than answering from scratch and ignoring prior knowledge. Reflection becomes the precondition for response.

02

Show the Seams

HIGH / MODERATE / UNCERTAIN tags on every paragraph. Source attribution made visible, clickable, honest. The system admits what it doesn't know — directly countering the fluency heuristic and automation bias.

03

Grow With You

Revenue tied to users improving — not engaging more. A subscription that succeeds when you need the tool less over time. Aligned with the Donut Model: regenerative, not extractive.

03 — The Approach
03— Testing Method

Five participants.
Five life contexts.

Persona-based walkthroughs with participants representing diverse, non-design backgrounds. The brief was simple: expose Mentor to people whose decisions have real legal, professional, and creative consequences — and see whether the principles hold.

Sessions ran 28–36 minutes with a 3-day follow-up check-in. Names are pseudonyms; data is anonymised. Every session followed the same structure: baseline → walkthrough → reaction → behaviour prediction → context closer.

5
Participants
28–36m
Session length
3d
Follow-up window
5/5
Success criteria met
04 — Prototype
04— What Participants Saw

The mechanisms,
made tangible.

Four flows. Three principles. Every screen is the thesis applied to pixels.

— Flow 01 / Onboarding

Intent before interaction.

"An AI that grows with you, not for you" — the tagline sets cognitive partnership as the expectation from first view. No productivity promises. Users choose their mode before the tool responds: Learn Deeply, Work Efficiently, or Balance Both. The first reflective moment is not preference collection — it is the user stating intent before the tool reacts.

"Begin Thinking" — deliberate copy. Not "Get Started." Language reinforces Mentor as a thinking tool, not a query processor.

"That's different. Usually it just says ask me anything."— Ash, ECE Educator
— Flow 01 / The Core Mechanism

The pause is the product.

Before generating any response, Mentor asks: "What's your instinct on this before we explore it together?" The user types their own thinking first. The AI then builds on what they already know rather than answering from scratch.

Share and Skip carry equal visual weight. Optional, never blocking — the invitation to reflect remains, but coercion is designed out.

"I didn't realize I already had ideas — I just never stopped to think about them."— Ash, ECE Educator
— Flow 02 / Show the Seams

Epistemic honesty as interface.

Three tiers per paragraph. Solid amber border for HIGH. Dashed for MODERATE — verify before relying. Dotted for UNCERTAIN — the system is speculating; apply independent judgment. The AI admits what it doesn't know — the most radical departure from standard AI behaviour, and the most-valued feature across all 5 sessions.

"ChatGPT never says it doesn't know. One time I almost missed a deadline because of that."— Tariq, Warehouse Worker
— Flow 02 / Source Attribution

Trust, judged at a glance.

Each response paragraph links to attribution — peer-reviewed, institutional, or academic sources. Clickable, transparent, honest. Users judge reliability visually rather than accepting confident tone as proof of accuracy. Counters the Fluency Heuristic identified in foundational research.

"This tells me which parts to check. My professors always say verify your sources."— Devon, Business Student
— Flow 03 / Deep Work Mode

Write your thinking first.

The AI panel is locked until the user writes at least 20 characters of original thought. A live counter ("0/20") removes all ambiguity about when AI activates. Calm Technology in practice — AI stays in the periphery, available but not leading. The work belongs to the user.

"This is the first AI tool that made me feel like the work is still mine."— Sahil, Web Developer
— Flow 04 / The Growth Dashboard

A new metric for AI success.

Four metrics — Origination Ratio, Reflection Rate, Recall Score, Deep Work Streak. Revenue tied to these improving, not to usage volume. An 8-week trend line tracks Cognitive Engagement, with annotated milestones. The standard AI business model, inverted.

"Score implies pass/fail. History implies journey."— Priya, Design Student → Renamed to "Your Thinking History"
05 — Voices
05— Five Voices

Five participants.
Five distinct responses.

The same prototype produced five genuinely different meaningful responses. Tariq's UNCERTAIN moment was about preventing real harm. Ash's nudge was about professional self-trust. Sahil's Deep Work was about career identity. Priya's dashboard critique was about product design. Devon's discovery was about academic credibility.

01
Ash
24 · ECE Educator
Mode: Balance Both
"I didn't realize I already had ideas — I just never stopped to think about them."
Used the nudge with a parent at work — asked herself the question before she spoke. "Weird how a computer thing made me do that."
02
Devon
22 · Business Student
Mode: Work Efficiently
"I use ChatGPT like a faster Google. This made me feel like I was actually thinking."
Wrote an essay using ChatGPT, didn't remember what he'd said the next day. Wondered if Mentor would have helped him remember.
03
Tariq
22 · Warehouse Worker
Mode: Learn Deeply
"This one tells me when it's not sure. That is the most important thing to me."
Looked up an NOC code on IRCC because of an UNCERTAIN tag. Found something ChatGPT had told him wrong before. The single most significant validation of the entire project.
04
Sahil
23 · Web Developer
Mode: Learn Deeply
"This is the first AI tool that made me feel like the work is still mine. I'd pay for this."
Started writing a 100-word outline before every prompt — manually, without the tool. The habit stuck even without the scratchpad.
05
Priya
23 · Design Student
Mode: Balance Both
"This is the first tool that named that feeling and tried to do something about it."
Used Mentor for a branding brief. The nudge slowed her down in a way she hated and then liked. Ended up with a better brief. Proposed the exact rename: "Your Thinking History."
06 — Findings
06— Synthesis

What the testing
actually proved.

5/5
F01 · Highest-value feature

Confidence indicators were the most-valued feature in every single session.

4 of 5 participants described them as addressing a real problem they had lived. Tariq's UNCERTAIN moment — directly contradicting wrong information he had previously acted on — was the single most significant validation of the entire project. This feature alone justifies Mentor's existence.

4/5
F02 · Mechanism right, frequency wrong

The nudge worked — when participants actually read it.

4/5 engaged meaningfully. But the nudge fired on every prompt with no frequency logic. All five either skipped on reflex or flagged it would become noise. The mechanism was right. The frequency was wrong.

3/3
F03 · The framing was the problem

"Score implies pass/fail. History implies journey."

Sahil wanted professional development data. Ash found it felt like a report card. Priya proposed the rename. All three were interested in the data — only the framing was wrong. Renamed to "Your Thinking History."

3/5
F04 · Trust gap on credibility

Static dashboard data broke the core promise.

Three participants asked why their numbers hadn't changed after their session. Static mockup data is a significant trust gap on a feature that promises to track real growth. Fixed with an honest "Sample data" label in the revised prototype.

5/5
F05 · Thesis validated

Non-design users validated the core thesis.

Ash trusted her own expertise more after one session. Tariq found wrong information ChatGPT had given him. Sahil adopted Thinking First independently. Priya produced a better brief. Real behavioural shifts from a single session.

07 — Honest Audit
— A flaw, surfaced honestly

The mobile keyboard hid the chat input.

When the keyboard opened, the input scrolled behind it. Users could not see what they were typing. Critical usability failure on the device most participants actually use — Tariq and Ash both work primarily from phones.

The transparent admission of prototype flaws was itself a design value. The case study lists every confirmed flaw with severity, status, and rationale.

✓ Fixed

visualViewport resize listener repositions the input above the keyboard dynamically. Confirmed resolved in the revised prototype.

08 — The Full Audit
08— 7 Confirmed Flaws

Documented
honestly.

Transparency about what a prototype can't do is itself a design value. Every flaw listed. Every fix tracked.

F1
Critical
Growth Score is static mockup data — doesn't update with real sessions.
✓ FixedRenamed to "Your Thinking History" + "Sample data" label
F2
High
Reflective nudge fires on every single prompt — no frequency logic.
✓ FixedFires once per session; ambient reminder thereafter
F3
Low
Knowledge cutoff early August — free-tier API limitation.
— ConstraintAPI tier limit, outside prototype control
F4
Med
No voice / mic input for users who think better by speaking.
⚠ UI mockupWeb Speech API blocked in iframe sandbox
F5
Med
No chat history — every session started blank, no continuity.
✓ FixedFull conversation history passed to API
F6
Med
Deep Work scratchpad input didn't auto-expand with content.
✓ FixedJS auto-resize on input event
F7
High
Mobile keyboard hides chat input — user can't see what they're typing.
✓ FixedvisualViewport listener applied
5/7
Flaws resolved in revised prototype
1/7
UI mockup — sandbox API constraint
1/7
External API tier constraint
09 — Iteration
09— Refined Screens

From feedback
to fix.

Three of seven flaws, resolved with visible care.

F2 · Frequency Logic

Once per session, with soft friction.

Nudge fires on the first prompt only. Skip tooltip — "Most people find sharing even a rough instinct changes the quality of the response they get." Honest, never blocking.

F6 · Auto-Expand + Rename

"Ready — your thinking will shape this response."

Counter turns amber at threshold. Textarea auto-resizes. Nav renamed from "Growth Score" to "Your Thinking History" — directly implementing Priya's feedback across every interface state.

— Closing the Loop

"Reasoned from your frame."

New badge on AI responses when Deep Work was active. The AI explicitly acknowledges the user's thinking first. Makes the value of the scratchpad visible in the output — the loop closes.

09.5— Try It Live

Now try it
yourself.

Everything documented above is real and working. The reflective nudge, confidence indicators, Deep Work mode — all live.

LIVE PROTOTYPE Open  ↗
Try Mentor live
10 — Reflection
10— After the Test

The shift that
surprised me most.

I built Mentor thinking about designers and students. Tariq and Ash showed me the stakes are higher for people whose decisions have real legal and professional consequences. That is the design brief I should have been writing from the start.

Designing Mentor required going slow. The act of designing against automatic AI use made me more deliberate about my own use. I now notice the reflex before I act on it. There's a small pause where there wasn't one before.

"Thriving in an automated world requires the active protection of cognitive integrity."

The honest caveat still holds: the real test is longitudinal. One session cannot prove cognitive growth. What it can prove is that the mechanism is felt. That feeling is the precondition for everything else.

From completion speed → To cognitive growth.
